---
title: "Metainformation"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{initialise_a_ts_archive"}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---
<!-- TODO: check if this does global eval = FALSE -->
```{r include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>",
  eval = FALSE
)
```

This site explains how to add data descriptions to your time series archive and in meanigful and efficient - yet simple - fashion. 
We recommend to write meta information after you [initialized your archive](initialise_a_ts_archive.Rmd) including import of the vintage history you want to start with. 


## General Naming Convention

In a researcher focused approach, the opentimeseries initiative suggests time series identifiers that are unique, speaking and hierarchical as opposed to hashes that are solely unique but hard to remember. 
Semantically meaningful identifiers make data analysis and interactive with data more convenient, e.g., when column names can be understood right away instead of needing lookup tables. 
Our suggestions are SDMX (Statistical Data and Meta Data Exchange), but avoid the overhead, maintenance and the overall complexity of SDMX. In general our keys follow a pattern like this: 

country.provider.dataset.frequency.variable.unit

ch.kof.

semantically meaningful chunks 



## Dataset Specific Information


## Variable Specific
